Finally, compared with the traditional learning path, the experimental results show that the BF-GN method has significant advantages in improving the optimization accuracy of the learning path model. Especially in the systematic transformation of fragmented learning resources, BF-GN method can more accurately generate systematic learning paths, reduce redundancy and repetition in the learning process, and improve learning efficiency. This fully validates the feasibility and effectiveness of this architecture in the optimization of AI enabled learning path model based on knowledge graph.

title: A Novel 1-Bit Adder Cell and Filters for Oversampling Superconducting ADCs
authors: Patricia Gonzalez-Guerrero, Panagiotis Zarkos, Carl Grace, George Michelogiannakis
publish: 2026 IEEE 17th Latin America Symposium on Circuits and System (LASCAS)
abstract: One of the major engineering challenges that applications that rely on cryogenic refrigeration, e.g., superconducting sensors or quantum computing, face are complex readout schemes. Advances in superconducting classical computing attempt to address these challenges by enabling us to place all the services provided by room temperature electronics into the cryogenic environment. Superconducting ADCs are one of the key elements to make this possible. In this work, we propose a novel 1-bit adder cell and evaluate the building blocks for the digital signal processing necessary for superconducting ADCs. We find that our DSP blocks consume 50% less power and area than state-of-the-art.

title: Adaptive Adjustment Algorithm for Backpack Shoulder Straps Based on User Gait Analysis
authors: Yongyu Chen
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power, Electrical Engineering, Electronics and Control (PEEEC)
abstract: Aiming at the problem that traditional backpack shoulder straps cannot be adaptively adjusted, this paper proposes an adaptive adjustment algorithm for backpack shoulder straps based on dynamic time warping (DTW) and reinforcement learning. The user's gait data and backpack pressure data are collected through the inertial measurement unit and the pressure sensor. The DTW algorithm is used to accurately match the user's personalized gait pattern, and the shoulder strap adjustment decision model is constructed in combination with reinforcement learning. An experimental platform was built, and 30 volunteers were recruited to conduct experiments in scenes such as flat ground, slopes, and stairs at different speeds of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.2 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}-1.8 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$</tex> to obtain 1000 sets of valid data. The experimental results show that compared with the traditional PID adjustment algorithm and the adjustment algorithm based on a single threshold, when walking on complex terrain, this algorithm reduces the backpack pressure fluctuation amplitude by 42.3 % and the shoulder strap pressure unevenness by 38.7 %, in the speed switching scenario, the adjustment response time is shortened to 0.8 s, an increase of 60 %, and the user's subjective comfort score is improved by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{2 7. 5 \%}$</tex>. Research shows that the algorithm significantly improves the comfort and stability of backpacks, and has important application value in the field of smart wearables.

title: Two-layer Hybrid XGBoost based on Sample Nearest Neighbor Enveloping Transposition Projection Mechanism
authors: Zhixuan Fan, Jie Ma, Tao Zhou, Xiaoping Zeng
publish: 2026 International Conference on Communication Networks and Machine Learning (CNML)
abstract: XGBoost is a widely recognized and efficient ensemble learning algorithm. However, existing methods commonly train classifiers directly on the original samples without effectively considering the correlation information between them, leading to limited accuracy in cases of low sample separability. To address this, we propose a novel ’two-layer hybrid XG-Boost’ model based on the Sample Nearest Neighbor Enveloping Transposition Projection Mechanism (SNNEPM). This model constructs a two-layer sample space to train and fuse two separate XGBoost models. First, the nearest neighbor samples of each current sample are calculated based on manifold distance. Second, a sample envelope is constructed and reconstructed into a new envelope sample via transposition projection. Finally, two XGBoost models are trained on the original and envelope samples, respectively, and their predictions are fused. Experiments on 10 public datasets show that our method improves ACC by an average of 2% compared to using only the original sample space, and by an average of 1.54% compared to state-of-the-art XGBoost variants, demonstrating its effectiveness.

title: Sound From the Air by Optical Tracking in Real Time
authors: Mian Xing, Alexei Sourin
abstract: This paper presents a hand-tracking-based digital Theremin that integrates Unity3D real-time audio synthesis with Leap Motion Controller 2 optical tracking. The system maps 3D hand positions to core musical parameters, pitch and volume, while allowing real-time control of tone type, waveform blending, reverb, and octave through additional spatial dimensions. A modular architecture combines continuous hand-tracking input, visual feedback, and procedural audio processing within a responsive user interface. Visual guidance panels support intuitive learning by displaying real-time feedback for pitch and volume control. A user study with both musicians and non-musicians demonstrated that the system was learnable, expressive, and responsive, though limitations in tracking precision and sound smoothing were observed. A user evaluation indicates that the system provides responsive and expressive control, and is learnable for both musicians and non-musicians.

title: ContrastFormer-NPI: A Sequence-Based Framework for NPIs Prediction via Representation-Based Feature Interaction
authors: Yifan Li, Wei Li, Peng Zhang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Communication Networks and Machine Learning (CNML)
abstract: Interactions between non-coding RNAs and proteins (NPIs) are pivotal in diverse biological processes and disease mechanisms. While computational methods utilizing molecular structure and graph topology are widely used for NPI prediction, they often struggle to achieve higher accuracy due to their dependence on prior knowledge and their inability to capture correlation information between NPI sequences. In this paper, we introduce a sequence-based NPI prediction model that integrates Longformer with contrastive learning. Our model utilizes Longformer to extract features from long biological sequences and employs the representation-based method for feature interaction. Furthermore, the InfoNCE loss is applied during training to enhance the quality of feature embeddings. Our model achieved accuracies of 88.7%, 98.0%, and 95.1% on the RPI488, RPI1807, and NPInter2.0 datasets, respectively. Experiments demonstrate that our model is comparable to or better than several state-of-the-art NPI prediction methods on both large-scale and small-scale datasets. This superior performance validates the effectiveness of our proposed model.

title: Token Energy Optimization (Semantic Cost Proxy) in GPT-2 Using Counterfactual Semantic Budgets: MetabolicGPT-2
authors: Abhijit Nayak, Raj Bhowmik
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Information and Communication (ICAIIC)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in settings where generation quality must be balanced against strict resource or verbosity constraints. Existing approaches to constrained text generation typically operate at decoding time, or optimize coarse proxies such as output length, with limited visibility into the semantic importance of individual tokens. In this work, we present MetabolicGPT-2, a GPT-2-based language model that learns an explicit token-level semantic cost proxy and generates under user-specified semantic budgets. Each token is assigned a non-negative cost that combines a corpus-driven synthetic prior with a counterfactual semantic cost (CSC) computed from a sentence encoder by measuring embedding change when a token is removed. We cast budget control as constrained optimization: minimize language modeling loss subject to an expected total proxy-cost constraint. We implement this by augmenting GPT-2 with a cost head, learnable token-cost parameters, a Lagrangian dual variable, and an online affine calibration layer that aligns proxy costs to CSC at the sequence level. Experiments on WikiText-2 illustrate that learned costs correlate with semantic importance and that budget settings shift the model toward lower-cost continuations while largely preserving fluency. Throughout, the term “energy” refers to this semantic proxy cost rather than direct hardware joules.

title: Semantic Integrity Preserving Recommendation Framework with Large Language Models
authors: Rongbing Niu, Yunfeng Fan, Haibo Ye
abstract: Recommendation systems based on deep learning and graph neural networks have effectively captured complex user-item relationships but struggle to fully leverage textual data for personalized recommendations. This is primarily due to three key challenges: (1) shallow semantic integration in language model representations, (2) semantic degradation during the fusion of large language model (LLM) embeddings with collaborative filtering signals, and (3) scalability bottlenecks in sparse data and large-scale interaction scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose SIP-Rec, a Semantic Integrity Preserving Recommendation Framework based on LLM and contrastive learning. First, SIP-Rec utilizes LLM to generate semantically rich profiles, accurately capturing fine-grained differences in user preferences and item characteristics. Second, it introduces an innovative cross-modal fusion mechanism that employs a dynamic alignment strategy to bridge the representation space gap between high-dimensional LLM embeddings and collaborative filtering signals, effectively mitigating contextual information loss. Finally, by integrating knowledge distillation with contrastive learning objectives, SIP-Rec optimizes the consistency between semantically rich representations and interaction patterns.

title: Structure-Aware and Implicit Restoration for Low-Light Light Field Image Compression
authors: Yuan Shi, Jimin Wang, Yiren Chen, Xianyang Wang, Deyang Liu
abstract: With the wide application of imaging technologies in low-light scenarios such as night surveillance and astronomical observation, low-light light field (LF) image has become increasingly critical. However, such imaging data face dual challenges. First, the multi-view structure inherently requires high storage and transmission bandwidth, as well as efficient compression. Second, under limited illumination conditions, the reconstructed images exhibit significant noise, low contrast, and detail loss, which severely compromise enhancement performance. Current approaches typically process compression and enhancement sequentially, which suffers from two critical drawbacks: (a) compression artifacts tend to eliminate high-frequency components essential for subsequent enhancement, and (b) existing enhancement algorithms demonstrate limited robustness when handling heavily compressed images. To bridge this gap, we propose a joint compression-enhancement framework that integrates epipolar plane image (EPI) structure-aware modeling into the compression process and incorporates feature-guided restoration during the decoding stage, thereby optimizing the bitrate-distortion-perception trade-off. Specifically, the EPI dual-axis attention (EDA) module is proposed to exploit the horizontal and vertical EPI consistency structures extracted along sub-aperture image (SAI) sequences. Furthermore, the Decoder-side Implicit Detail Restoration (D-IDR) module is designed to restore detailed features. This restoration is enabled by continuous implicit mapping within the compressed latent space, which integrates coordinate-based positional encoding and frequency-domain feature modeling. Experiments on public low-light LF datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods, achieving up to 37.40 % bit-rate savings while maintaining superior structural preservation and perceptual quality.

title: Research on Photovoltaic Power Generation Prediction Model Based on Deep Learning
authors: Yuan Kai, Yi Le'an, Rao Shaozhou, Tang Lei, Yu Ying, Jin Jianguang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power, Electrical Engineering, Electronics and Control (PEEEC)
abstract: Under the background of rapid development of renewable energy, achieving high precision PV power forecast is key to ensure stable operation of power system. A new PV power forecast algorithm is proposed, which integrates improved transformer and spatiotemporal attention mechanism. By using adaptive location coding, it improves the multi-head attention mechanism so as to capture long sequence dependency. Combining temporal and temporal attention fusion module, the paper uses multiscale feature fusion layer to enrich feature expression. Experiments were carried out using real PV data sets. The results show that RMSE, mean absolute error (MAE), determination coefficient (R2) were used as evaluation indicators in comparison with ARIMA, LSTM, GRU and traditional transformer algorithm. In short term forecast, this algorithm has a RMSE of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{0. 0 8 2}$</tex>, lower by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 8. 8 \%}$</tex> compared with best comparison algorithm; MAE is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.065,21.7 \%$</tex>; R2 reaches <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.921,8.3 \%$</tex> higher. RMSE of 0.053, MAE of 0.041, R2 of 0.956 in ultra-short term prediction scenario are significantly better than that of comparing algorithms. In addition, under different weather conditions, this algorithm can maintain good prediction stability. It is proved that this algorithm is practical and innovative in PV power generation prediction.

title: A Closed-Loop Framework for Autonomous Vehicle Safety: Root Cause Analysis, Enhanced Sensor Fusion, and Simulation Validation
authors: Zhiyuan Wen
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power, Electrical Engineering, Electronics and Control (PEEEC)
abstract: Ensuring the reliability of the perception-decision-making chain is crucial for autonomous vehicle safety. Current research often focuses on technical failures, lacking a systematic framework covering root cause analysis, solution design, and validation. To address this, we propose a closedloop framework integrating a three-layer (Technical-ProcessManagement) root cause model with an Enhanced Multisensor Redundancy System (EMSRS). EMSRS features a dynamic triggering mechanism that activates millimeter-wave radar under uncertainty, using a 2 -out-of-3 voting fusion logic and a dynamic safe distance model. Extensive simulations on CARLA show our approach outperforms baselines, with significant reductions in Risk Priority Number (from 180 to 65), pedestrian miss detection rate (33% decrease), and emergency braking events (58% reduction), while achieving 100% path planning success. Ablation studies confirm each component's contribution. This work offers a holistic solution for AV safety from risk identification to mitigation.

title: Greedy Analytics for Card Rewards: A Data-Driven Portfolio Optimizer With Simulation-Backed ROI Insights
authors: Dhanush Gopal Battina
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Sentiment Analysis and Deep Learning (ICSADL)
abstract: This paper develops and deploys a business analytics pipeline to optimize consumer credit-card portfolios by maximizing net rewards subject to annual fees, category caps, and redemption regimes. A greedy selection algorithm with dynamic programming elements operationalizes an empirically specified objective over category spend, card multipliers, redemption valuations, static benefits, and costs, producing interpretable, incremental marginal value at each portfolio expansion. External data integration aligns household spend with income-stratified budgets, and large-scale Monte Carlo sampling quantifies expected return on spend, dispersion, and sensitivity to user preferences and benefit utilization. The implemented decision app (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}$</tex> Shiny) translates model outputs into actionable recommendations and category-level card assignments for end users. Results reveal diminishing marginal benefits beyond a small portfolio size and heterogeneous ROI driven by spend mix and benefit realization, illustrating how simulation and optimization inform product selection decisions in consumer finance analytics.

title: Enhancing Digital Image Forensics: a Comparative Analysis on Deep Learning Frameworks for Copy-Move Forgery Detection
authors: E.M. Suneth Piyumantha, A.A.S.S. Pieris, P.M.A.M. Pathiraja, J.M. Sandamali, K.D.G.I.M. Wijekoon, J.M.D.S. Jayasinghe, W.A.W.U. Wickramaarachchi
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Advanced Research in Computing (ICARC)
abstract: The rapid development of digital image manipulation technologies has made image forgery detection a critical area of research in cyber forensics. Copy-move forgery is one of the most common forms of image manipulation. This involves copying and pasting a region within the same image to hide or duplicate the original content. Traditional detection methods are often ineffective in detecting geometric transformations done on images such as scaling, rotation, and compression. Deep learning technologies have significantly advanced image forensics. However, there is limited research that compares different neural network architectures used for copy-move forgery detection. This study presents a comprehensive analysis of five deep learning models (CNN, GAN, RNN, ResNet50, and VGG16) evaluated on three benchmark datasets to identify their effectiveness in detecting copy-move forgeries. Each deep learning model consists of unique features and capabilities, thus enabling a detailed investigation of their strengths, limitations, and suitability for various manipulation scenarios. Preprocessing steps included normalization, rotation, scaling, and contrast enhancement to ensure robustness. We evaluated the impact of different model parameters on each selected deep learning model. Experimental results show that CNN achieved the highest training accuracy (93.8 %). The VGG16 and ResNet50 outperformed (88 % and 80 % respectively), but not higher than the CNN. The outcomes provide valuable insights about the influence of architectural design on copy-move forgery detection performance. Also contribute toward guiding future forensic model development.

title: Research Review on the Core Technological System of the Metaverse and the Virtual-Real Integration Mechanism
authors: Yibei Wang, Mingtao Zhou, Junfeng Zhao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Communication Networks and Machine Learning (CNML)
abstract: As an emerging form of cyber–physical integration system that fuses multiple digital technologies, the metaverse has attracted extensive attention from both academia and industry in recent years. Existing studies largely focus on isolated analyses of individual technologies, with limited in-depth discussion of the interdependencies among technologies and the challenges of system-level integration. Building upon a synthesis of relevant domestic and international research, this paper concentrates on the metaverse’s core technological stack—extended reality, digital twins, and artificial intelligence—and their integration mechanisms. It places particular emphasis on identifying critical bottlenecks in cross-technology collaboration and provides a systematic review of the state of research in each technological domain. By developing a framework for analyzing technological interrelations, this paper aims to offer theoretical references for advancing the metaverse from a collection of component technologies toward a systems-engineering paradigm.

title: Emotion-Based Classification of TikTok Videos Using CNNs and Hashtag Generation via RAG-Enhanced LLMs
authors: S.D. Nagodavithana, R.A.H.M. Rupasingha, B. T. G. S. Kumara
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Advanced Research in Computing (ICARC)
abstract: Emotion recognition, an AI-driven computer vision technology, allows software to read interpersonal expressions through visual clues, and in the context of a rapidly growing short-video platform like TikTok, emotional expression and hashtags created by users offer important insights into human behavior in the real world and within social media interactions. TikTok poses significant challenges to automated analysis because of the uncontrolled lighting, fast motion, varying user actions, and audio-visual noise. To address these complexities, the objective of this study is to propose a hybrid model consisting of a CNN-based emotion recognition system and a Retrieval-Augmented LLM (RAG-LLM)–based emotion sensitive hashtag generation unit. Four major emotion categories namely happiness, sadness, anger, and surprise commonly adopted in prior multimedia-based emotion detection studies were selected, and a total of 4,000 TikTok videos were collected and preprocessed. Then input as frames to three CNN models namely MobileNet, VGGNet, and ResNet. The experimental findings indicate that ResNet recorded the best performance (96 % accuracy and high precision, recall, F1score, and minimum error rates). Video transcripts and detected emotions were further ranked by an RAG pipeline with the help of a Sentence Transformer and FAISS video storage to access semantically similar samples to guide three lightweight LLMs, namely DistilGPT-2, GPT-Neo, and FLAN-T5. The comparison of performance based on Precision@k, Recall@k, and F1@k shows that FLAN-T5 generates the most contextually relevant and emotion-consistent hashtags. Altogether, this research paper suggests a unified multimodal framework that interpolates CNN-based emotion recognition with smart RAG-enhanced hashtag generation, which would add to the correct and emotion-sensitive analysis of short videos on social networks.

title: A Framework for Stroke Prediction Using CDA-ResNet with Adaptive Feature Selection and Evaluation of Ensemble ML Methods
authors: Talekar Rohini Rustum, P Praveen, Mohammed Ali Shaik
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Sentiment Analysis and Deep Learning (ICSADL)
abstract: This study provides a stroke prediction framework with CDA-ResNet with adaptive feature selection and a comparison of the performance of different ensemble machine learning (ML) approaches. The CDA-ResNet model is the second model that can improve the accuracy of the prediction process by using the dense convolutional blocks and attention to extract meaningful features out of the brain MRI images. Adaptive feature selection is the best approach to optimization of the model as it picks the most relevant features and minimizes overfitting and efficiency. Ensemble such as Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM are compared and evaluated on the framework to determine its performance. Based on the results obtained, the proposed CDA-ResNet model, with adaptive feature selection, has an accuracy of 94.5 % and F1-score of 0.93 and AUC of 0.97, which compares with the traditional models. The ensemble techniques also enhance the performance and XGBoost has the best precision and recall. This framework has a great potential of detecting early strokes which is a reliable instrument of clinical decision making and intervention planning.

title: Vision-Controllable Language Model for Image-Guided Story Ending Generation
authors: Dizhan Xue, Shengsheng Qian, Changsheng Xu
abstract: Despite the unparalleled capabilities of recently developed large language models (LLMs), controllable natural language generation (NLG) is still a tricky and important problem that aims at satisfying specific constraints in NLG to facilitate practical applications. Moreover, various severe limitations compared to small language models, including applicability, responsiveness, and environmental impact, also prevent the application of LLMs in many practical scenarios. Therefore, in this work, we explore Image-guided Story Ending Generation (IgSEG) with small language models, which requires the agent to respond to a multimodal environment and generate text following visual controls. Accordingly, we propose Vision-Controllable Language Model (VCLM) which can integrate cross-modal inference ability into both trained-from-scratch and pretrained language models. Firstly, we devise a multimodal-contextual cloud knowledge retrieval, which periodically retrieves textual knowledge from a cloud LLM based on the multimodal query. Secondly, we design a multimodal prototype condensation module to condense key information in multimodal dependent representations. Thirdly, text-only LM layers are interleaved with cross-modal intervention layers to infuse multimodal information. Finally, we propose vision-controlled reinforcement learning to explicitly constrain our model to follow visual controls. In experiments, extensive automatic evaluation results and human evaluation results indicate the significant performance improvements of our proposed VCLM over state-of-the-art methods on two benchmark datasets. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/LivXue/VCNLG</uri>.

title: A Low-Latency MTPA Control Strategy Based on Dual Virtual Signal Injection for IPMSM
authors: Dongjin Wang, Zhonggang Yin, Yanping Zhang, Yixuan Gao, Qi Liu
abstract: Since the existence of low-pass filters (LPF) in the conventional virtual signal injection control, the system suffers from delays and inaccurate maximum-torque-per-ampere (MTPA) trajectories. To tackle the issues of dynamic response lag and MTPA trajectory deviation, this paper proposes a current vector angle determination strategy based on dual virtual signal injection (DVSI). The DVSI method superimposes two sine and cosine waves of identical frequency onto the measured current angle signal. Using the Taylor expansion and superposition principle, the proposed method cancels high-frequency components in the torque derivative to realize direct extraction of the torque derivative signal, thereby avoiding delays and errors introduced by LPF. The experiments conducted on a 3.0 kW interior permanent-magnet synchronous machine (IPMSM) drive platform validated the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: ConSSAC: Conservative Sigmoidal Soft Actor-Critic for Offline Dialogue Policy Optimization
authors: Fatemeh Shamsezat, Saeed Shiry Ghidary, Ali Mohades
abstract: Optimizing dialogue policies in multi-domain task-oriented systems is challenging because of the limited opportunities for real user interaction. Pipeline-based architectures offer efficiency and interpretability; however, their dependence on user simulators limits scalability and generalization to real-world conditions. Offline reinforcement learning has emerged as a promising alternative to reduce simulator reliance. However, offline settings amplify inherent difficulties, including a distribution shift and Q-value overestimation when training on imperfect data. We propose Conservative Sigmoidal Soft Actor–Critic (ConSSAC), an offline reinforcement learning method for discrete dialogue action spaces. ConSSAC extends Sigmoidal Soft Actor–Critic (SSAC) by integrating a conservative Q-learning objective for actions that are weakly supported or unobserved in the dataset, while retaining the sigmoidal policy architecture for calibrated multi-label action selection. This design enables the reliable exploitation of indistribution behaviors. In addition, we introduce RewardWOZ-21, a turn-level reward–annotated extension of MultiWOZ 2.1, which enables scalable and fine-grained offline training. Experiments on ConvLab-3 indicate that ConSSAC achieves higher performance than SSAC, PPO-based models, and recent LLM-based policies under the evaluated settings. It achieves a 4.1% absolute improvement in F1 by reducing false positives and false negatives in dialogue-act selection, while preserving computational efficiency comparable to that of standard actor–critic architectures. These results suggest that combining conservative offline reinforcement learning with sigmoidal policy parameterization is associated with improved robustness and reliability in multi-domain task-oriented dialogue systems.

title: Information Experience: The Strategy and Tactics of Design Thinking: Craig Baehr: [Book Review]
authors: James Berryhill
abstract: Presents reviews for the following list of books, Information Experience: The Strategy and Tactics of Design Thinking.

title: Design and Experimental Validation of a Solid-State Pulse Generator With 12-kV, 120-A Output for Switched-Oscillator Excitation
authors: Fredrick T. Nkado, Hao Huang, Hang Zhou, Fatemeh Babaeian, John E. Fletcher, J. Scott Tyo
abstract: This article presents the design and experimental validation of a solid-state high-voltage (HV) pulse generator to study the influence of excitation characteristics on switched-oscillator (SWO) performance. The generator is based on a series–parallel switch module architecture that integrates two parallel series semiconductor switch modules, each comprising five series-connected 1.2-kV SiC MOSFETs, driven by a single-isolated gate driver with coupling gate-drive capacitors, eliminating the need for individual drivers. The symmetrical arrangement of the two series semiconductor switch modules ensures balanced current sharing and synchronized operation within each stage. The stages are assembled in a coaxial structure to minimize the output pulse distortion. The three-stage design delivers 12kV into a 100-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Omega $</tex-math> </inline-formula> load, with a peak current of 120A, a 15-ns rise time, and an instantaneous power of 1.44MW at a 1-kHz repetition rate. The generator demonstrated flexible operation with adjustable pulse widths (300–600ns), and controllable rise times (15–110ns) and fall times (32–158ns) through the sequential stage switching. Performance metrics are 48-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\cdot $</tex-math> </inline-formula>kV <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\cdot $</tex-math> </inline-formula> A per switch and 53-V/ns per-device slew rate, demonstrating competitive performance relative to recent solid-state pulsed generator designs. Based on this foundation, the design is intended to be scalable up to 50kV in future implementations to expand its capacity for high-power electromagnetic (HPEM) applications.

title: Fixed-Time ADRC for Enhanced Load Frequency Regulation of Interconnected Power System
authors: Inam Ul Haq, Syed Muhammad Amrr, Mohamed Zaery, Asadur Rahman, S. M. Suhail Hussain, Mohammad A. Abido
abstract: With the rapid integration of renewable energy sources (RES), maintaining fast and stable load frequency control (LFC) in interconnected power systems has become increasingly challenging due to the uncertain nature of the system and the unreliability of RES. In contrast to existing asymptotic or finite-time stable active disturbance rejection control (ADRC) for the LFC problem, this paper proposes a novel fixed-time ADRC (FxT-ADRC) scheme for a two-area interconnected hybrid power system. The controller incorporates a newly developed extended state observer (ESO) capable of estimating system states and lumped disturbances within a fixed time frame, regardless of the initial conditions. This ensures timely state estimation and disturbance compensation, while rapidly stabilizing frequency deviations of the two-area system to zero. The convergence of the ESO and the stability of the system states are rigorously established using Lyapunov theory, ensuring fixed-time convergence of both disturbance estimation and state errors. To improve performance, the Magnetotactic Bacteria Optimization (MBO) algorithm is applied for optimal tuning of the ADRC gain parameters, while the proportional gain matrix is derived by solving a linear matrix inequality. Simulation studies demonstrate that the proposed FxT-ADRC achieves superior performance compared to existing asymptotic and finite-time ADRC schemes, providing faster frequency restoration, shorter settling time, and reduced overshoot and undershoot. Moreover, the method exhibits strong robustness against load variations and communication delays, and its effectiveness is further validated through real-time implementation on the OPAL-RT platform.

title: Segment-wise Joint Pose and Parametric Geometry Estimation for Multi-Part Quasi-Conical and Cylindrical Objects
authors: Hao Wu, Lijuan Li, Aolei Yang, Zhile Yang
abstract: Accurate modeling of multi-part objects with diverse quasi-conical and cylindrical geometries requires a unified understanding of both their semantic composition and underlying geometric structure. To address this challenge, a joint Pose and Parametric Geometry Estimation (PPGE) framework is proposed, which integrates learned representations with explicit geometric priors for simultaneous segment-wise segmentation and parametric geometry prediction from raw point clouds. The proposed hybrid architecture employs an augmented PointNet++ backbone to extract multi-scale spatial features and a squeeze-and-channel-attention module to enhance inter-channel discriminability and context awareness. Three slot-based task-specific prediction heads are designed to separately infer segment membership, symmetry axis orientation, and point-wise surface normals, providing complementary semantic and geometric cues. These multi-branch outputs are further coupled by a geometrically-constrained probability-axis-normal fusion module, which enforces alignment between semantic segmentation, axis direction, and normal consistency, yielding coherent segment reconstructions even under partial or noisy observations. To refine parametric accuracy, a mode-climb parameter estimation module iteratively adjusts axis direction, height and radius parameters toward physically valid local optima. Experimental evaluations on multiple datasets of quasi-conical and cylindrical assemblies, along with physical prototype validation, demonstrate that PPGE achieves superior robustness and accuracy compared to baseline and ablated variants, particularly under challenging conditions of segment overlap, noise, and occlusion.

title: Equivalent Element Pattern-Assisted Heterogeneous Phased Array Antenna Design for Wide-Angle Scanning
authors: Kangna Zhang, Xue Ren
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Evaluating the impact of parameter optimization on the scanning performance of phased array antennas (PAAs) with heterogeneous beam elements (HBEs) is challenging. This letter proposes an Equivalent Element Pattern (EEP) method to address this issue. An HBE PAA with a beam-splitting lens (BSL) is designed, with the BSL parameters optimized using the EEP method. A fabricated 1×8 HBE PAA with BSL achieves an extended 3-dB scanning range from ±51° to ±90° compared with a standard element PAA, demonstrating the potential of this approach for wide-angle scanning enhancement.

title: CRIXP: Cultural Knowledge Representation for Inherent eXPlainability
authors: Abhisek Sharma, Sarika Jain
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Cultural awareness plays a vital role in making AI systems beneficial for a globalized audience. This cultural awareness is lacking in modern decision-support systems and in the current state of the work, where explainability is the paramount requirement now from all AI systems. To address this, the article introduces CRIXP, a framework with decisive cultural knowledge and the capability to explain the reasoning behind its suggestions. CRIXP comprises: 1) a cultural ontology (CRO); 2) a cultural knowledge graph (CKG); 3) a general-purpose adaptable algorithm providing recommendations and explanations; and 4) a CKG enrichment module. The cultural traits of all the locales are consolidated and represented as an ontology and a knowledge graph. A cultural shift suggestion system has been demonstrated for recommendations and explanations. The response generated is compared with ChatGPT. CRIXP is found to outperform ChatGPT by providing more relevant recommendations with inherent explainability.

title: CRIXP: Cultural Knowledge Representation for Inherent eXPlainability_supp1-3674361.pdf

title: G-MAPP: GPU-accelerated Multi-Agent Planning and Perception for Reactive Motion Generation
authors: Tanmay Bishnoi, Riddhiman Laha, Tobias Löw, Jose Alex Chandy, Luis F.C. Figueredo, Sami Haddadin
abstract: Reactive motion generation in unstructured environments remains an open challenge in robotics. Due to the computational complexity of collision-free motion generation, existing methods either generate global trajectories for static scenarios, or employ models that make conservative assumptions about the environment. This paper identifies the primary bottleneck as the runtime performance demand of planning on high-fidelity environments, and the temporal integration between the perception and planning modules. Therefore, we propose a framework that does not compromise on runtime performance and world representations for perception and planning by accelerating world modeling and vector-field based planning using the GPU. This allows us to achieve faster parallel state exploration for quasiglobal trajectory planning, and tighter coupling of the perceptionaction loop in real-time for dynamic cluttered environments with off-the-shelf depth sensors. We quantitatively evaluate the computation-time and success rate differences for the CPU and GPU versions of our planner, and perform qualitative evaluations of our coupled framework using real-world experiments on a 7- DoF Franka Emika robot. Experimental results demonstrate that our GPU-based framework achieves up to a 5x speedup over the CPU version and successfully avoids collisions across both trivial and challenging physical world scenarios. The implementation is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/chart-research/g-mapp</uri>

title: How Accurate Should Prediction Be for Connected Autonomous Vehicles at Blind Intersections?
authors: Lu Tao, Yousuke Watanabe, Shen Ying, Yuhuan Lu, Zhengshu Zhou, Hiroaki Takada
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Vehicle trajectory prediction (VTP) is a prominent research topic in autonomous vehicles (AVs). However, existing studies concentrate on improving prediction accuracy, overlooking how VTP accuracy influences the effectiveness of downstream tasks such as collision avoidance. In this paper, we investigate the relationship between VTP accuracy and key performance indicators (KPIs) of cooperative collision avoidance systems (CCAS) for connected AVs operating at blind intersections. To this end, we propose a generalized error model (GEM) that enables the generation of customizable VTP errors. GEM is integrated into an error injection framework to produce diverse, error-controlled trajectories in real time. Our experiments quantitatively characterize the relationship among VTP accuracy, warning thresholds and strategies, and CCAS KPIs. The results reveal a common pattern: as VTP error increases, CCAS recall improves while precision declines, demonstrating a negative correlation that is difficult to balance. This underscores the need for effective trade-offs between recall and precision in CCAS design. With a general collision warning strategy, CCAS can preemptively report over 90% of collisions, substantially enhancing traffic safety, though at the expense of a reduced precision. To mitigate this, we devise a density-based warning strategy (DCWS) that improves precision across the entire VTP accuracy space while maintaining recall at acceptable levels. Notably, DCWS gradually decouples CCAS KPIs from VTP accuracy performance, highlighting the crucial role of warning strategies. The quantitative results presented in this paper can help practitioners assess the impact of their VTP models on CCAS and select appropriate warning thresholds.

title: Microwave SQUID Multiplexer Readout Performance Using a Direct-RF RFSoC-Based Software-Defined Radio
authors: M. E. García Redondo, J. D. Bonilla Neira, N. A. Müller, L. P. Ferreyro, J. M. Geria, T. Muscheid, R. Gartmann, A. Almela, M. R. Hampel, L. Ardila-Perez, M. Wegner, M. Platino, O. Sander, S. Kempf, M. Weber
abstract: In this work, we report the experimental readout results of a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$µ$</tex-math></inline-formula> MUX device using a Direct-RF Software-Defined Radio (SDR) prototype based on the ZCU216 Radio-Frequency System-on-Chip (RFSoC) evaluation board. First, the Direct-RF SDR architecture is described. Then, the analog performance of the SDR system was characterized both in loopback and when coupled to the cryogenic multiplexing system to evaluate the noise contributions of the SDR readout system. Later, the SDR system performance was optimized for <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$µ$</tex-math></inline-formula> MUX readout, focusing on bolometric applications. Finally, the minimum demodulated flux noise was obtained through the optimally conditioned readout of a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$µ$</tex-math></inline-formula> MUX channel. The results obtained showed a minimum demodulated white flux noise of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\approx 4 \mu \Phi _{0}/\sqrt{Hz}$</tex-math></inline-formula> in both demodulation domains (i.e. amplitude <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\gamma$</tex-math></inline-formula> and resonator phase <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\theta$</tex-math></inline-formula>) and dominated by the cryogenic microwave chain noise temperature of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$T_{n}^{cryo}=4.6$</tex-math></inline-formula> K. These results are comparable to those obtained with traditional SDR architectures and demonstrate that the Direct-RF SDR prototype is suitable for the readout of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$µ$</tex-math></inline-formula> MUX devices.

title: Synthesis of Deep Neural Networks with Safe Robust Adaptive Control for Reliable Operation of Wheeled Mobile Robots
authors: Mehdi Heydari Shahna, Jouni Mattila
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) can enable precise control while maintaining low computational costs by circum-venting the need for dynamic modeling. However, the deployment of such black-box approaches remains challenging for heavy-duty wheeled mobile robots (WMRs), which are subject to strict international standards and prone to faults and disturbances. We designed a hierarchical control policy for heavy-duty WMRs, monitored by two safety layers with differing levels of authority. To this end, a DNN policy was trained and deployed as the primary control strategy, providing high-precision performance under nominal operating conditions. When external disturbances arise and reach a level of intensity such that the system performance falls below a predefined threshold, a low-level safety layer intervenes by deactivating the primary control policy and activating a model-free robust adaptive control (RAC) policy. This transition enables the system to continue operating while ensuring stability by effectively managing the inherent trade-off between system robustness and responsiveness. Regardless of the control policy in use, a high-level safety layer continuously monitors system performance during operation. It initiates a shutdown only when disturbances become sufficiently severe such that compensation is no longer viable and continued operation would jeopardize the system or its environment. The proposed synthesis of DNN and RAC policy guarantees uniform exponential stability of the entire WMR system while adhering to safety standards to some extent. The effectiveness of the proposed approach was further validated through real-time experiments using a 6,000 kg WMR.

title: Time Synchronized Deterministic 6G Middleware Supporting AI Driven English Education in Cyber Physical Systems
authors: Xiaohui Zhang, Jia Guo
abstract: The combination of artificial intelligence (AI), cyber-physical systems (CPS), and next-generation wireless communication is changing digital education, especially in language learning environments that need real-time interaction and feedback that changes based on it the learner is demonstrating. AI-powered English language learning platforms are increasingly using multimodal data streams, such as speech, gestures, eye gaze, and learner engagement metrics. These streams need precise timing, low latency, and communication guarantees that are constantly identical. But most middleware solutions lack the rigorous time synchronization and dependability that these interactive, CPS-enabled educational systems require. This study develops up a Time-Synchronized Deterministic 6G Middleware architecture intended to facilitate AI-driven English instruction within cyber-physical systems. This study develops a Time-Synchronized Deterministic 6G Middleware (TSD-6G-EDU) architecture intended to facilitate AI-driven English instruction within cyber-physical systems. The proposed middleware uses 6G-native features like ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC), sub-microsecond time synchronization, and edge intelligence to make it easy for physical learning devices to work with AI-based educational services. A deterministic scheduling method guarantees limited latency and jitter for essential learning interactions, including real-time voice evaluation, pronunciation correction, and adaptive tutoring feedback. The middleware uses AI to help manage resources and time-aware message orchestration to dynamically allocate network, computation, and sensor resources based on the learner’s context. The system keeps digital copies of learners and instructional processes in sync, which makes it possible to have consistent perception–action phases that are necessary for individualized and immersive English learning experiences. Performance assessments show that the proposed middleware greatly lowers end-to-end latency variance and improves synchronization accuracy compared to other middleware methods. The findings demonstrate that time-synchronized deterministic 6G middleware establishes an effective structure for next-generation AI-enhanced English teaching, adaptive, and context-sensitive learning within cyber-physical educational environments.

title: Reliability-Based Design and Control of Crystallization Systems Under Parametric and Process Uncertainties
authors: Yash G. Barhate, Zoltan K. Nagy
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: Population balance model-based approaches are widely employed in crystallization process design to meet the industry-specific critical quality attributes (CQAs). However, their reliability is often limited by parameter uncertainties arising from noisy experimental data, which if, unaccounted for, can lead to suboptimal or failed designs. This study introduces a novel reliability-based design optimization (RBDO) framework for open-loop design of crystallization systems under uncertainty. The framework formulates CQA constraints as probabilistic constraints with user-defined reliability thresholds, enabling quantified, risk-informed process design. To solve the resulting RBDO problems efficiently, a surrogate modeling-based, nested simulation-optimization workflow is developed. The proposed methodology is applied to both batch and continuous pharmaceutical crystallization systems, demonstrating its ability to generate robust design trajectories with significantly higher probabilities of meeting CQAs under both parametric and operational uncertainties compared to the deterministic designs. The computational efficiency and accuracy of the approach are further validated through benchmarking against back-mapping-based solutions of the underlying chance constrained formulations.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Letters on Electromagnetic Compatibility Practice and Applications

title: 6G SDVN: UAV-Assisted VANETs for AI-Enabled Controller Selection and Heterogeneous Multi-Band Connectivity
authors: Muhammad Rafid, Abid Afridi, Sara Sualiheen, Amjad Iqbal, Ihsan Ali, Adnan Akhunzada
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Road sparsity is a major cause of routing instability, leading to intermittent connectivity and increased packet loss. To enhance stability in 6G software-defined vehicular networks (SDVNs), we integrate unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) into a dynamic vehicular environment. We propose a novel ground-to-air communication protocol that integrates Dedicated Short Range Communication (DSRC), millimeter-wave (mmWave), and terahertz (THz) bands to address path stability, load balancing among controllers, and technology switching to accommodate diverse vehicular demands. Roadside Units (RSUs) and UAVs act as local controllers (LCs), with RSUs communicating via LTE and UAVs via DSRC. The optimal controller (OC) is selected using supervised learning algorithms, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Random Forest (RF), to ensure balanced load distribution and to manage stable routing across hybrid wireless technologies. The proposed scheme evaluates multiple UAV roles, including LC, relay, and destination, under different communication interfaces. Simulation results in a highway scenario demonstrate that the hybrid approach outperforms the reference DSRC, mmWave, and THz schemes in terms of packet delivery ratio, end-to-end delay, and routing overhead. Specifically, SVM-based OC selection achieved a maximum packet delivery ratio of 97.94%, with an end-to-end delay as low as 0.089 ms and minimal routing overhead, validating its effectiveness in mitigating road sparsity, improving connectivity, and enhancing network performance.

title: Assumption-Free Active Calibration of mm-Wave Phased Arrays Using Excitation Estimation via Far-Field Measurements
authors: Mohammad Abdollah Chalaki, Ahmed Ben Ayed, Patrick Mitran, Slim Boumaiza
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter proposes an active calibration method for millimeter-wave (mm-Wave) beamforming arrays that estimates the complex excitation of each element under realistic operating conditions. The method leverages a sequence of far-field transmission coefficient measurements collected while the array is rotated toward different angles. From these measurements, the complex excitation at each element is estimated without imposing any assumptions on the beamforming circuitry behavior. With all elements active during measurements, the estimated excitations inherently capture effects of mutual coupling and loading, which traditional elementwise calibration fails to account for. The method is validated on a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4{\,}\times {\,}4$</tex-math> </inline-formula> phased array operating at 38.5 GHz. Radiation patterns reconstructed from the estimated excitations match the measured patterns with a normalized root-mean-square error (NRMSE) better than −30 dB, confirming that the estimated excitations closely represent the true active-array behavior. In contrast, reconstructing the pattern using excitations obtained from elementwise measurements yields an NRMSE of only −8 dB. Furthermore, when beamforming toward boresight, the proposed active calibration method improves the measured boresight gain by 1.4 dB relative to passive calibration and reduces sidelobe-level imbalance by 2.4 dB. Finally, when the proposed method is applied for boresight null-steering, it achieves a null-depth that is 21.2 dB deeper than that obtained from passive calibration.

title: Performance Comparison of Cutting-Edge Deep Learning Architectures for DDoS Attack Detection on Large-Scale Network Data
authors: Prabjot Singh, Veenu Mangat
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Intelligent Network Computing (ICDSINC)
abstract: Intrusion detection systems (IDS), critical components in today's cybersecurity, are evolving in operational complexity as the demand of interconnecd systems is rising rapidly. Traditional IDS, for instance, signature-based IDS fail to detect novel threats, although they are highly effective in detecting previously identified attacks. With the proliferation of Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems, our existing network infrastructures are now more vulnerable to new types of attacks, thereby disrupting the availability of essential services, stealing sensitive information, or causing financial and reputational damage. This study presents a comparative analysis of various Deep Learning (DL) approaches to detect Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks, as these attacks pose a significant threat to network security by flooding target systems with malicious traffic aggregates, resulting in service unavailability. Here, we compared the performance of various DL models, including RNN, GRU, LSTM, BiLSTM, and CNN, which are trained using the CICIDS2017 and CICIDS2019 datasets. Our experiments demonstrated that BiLSTM achieved the highest detection accuracy, outperforming other models in terms of precision, recall, and F1-score. The results highlight that DL can provide a robust mechanism for enhancing the detection capabilities of modern IDS.

title: Spatial-temporal Scene Graph Generation for Open-Vocabulary Multiple Object Tracking
authors: Guangyao Li, Siping Zhuang, Yajun Jian, Yan Yan, Hanzi Wang
abstract: Open-vocabulary multiple object tracking (MOT) aims to track arbitrary objects in the real world. Although significant progress has been achieved in object classification by leveraging the knowledge from large vision-language models, advances in data association for open-vocabulary MOT remain limited. Existing methods primarily rely on appearance cues to establish associations. However, these cues are often unreliable in the face of occlusions and ambiguous object appearances, resulting in suboptimal tracking performance in complex scenarios. In this paper, we propose a novel open-vocabulary MOT method, Spatial-temporal Scene Graph Tracker (SSGTrack), which introduces a fundamentally different approach to data association by building a Spatial-temporal Scene Graph (SSG) that captures rich semantic and spatial relationships between objects across adjacent frames. Specifically, SSGTrack constructs proposal-level relationships by extracting diverse contextual information from the multi-head self-attention layers of the Transformer decoder. These relationships, derived from the keyframe and reference frame, are compressed into the compact SSG, where nodes represent detected objects, and edge weights denote frame-level connectivity. Furthermore, to address the challenge of differentiating visually similar objects and background distractors, we propose a Context-aware Contrastive Learning (CCL) strategy. By identifying background features that significantly differ from positive samples and incorporating them as negative samples, CCL enhances the ability of the model to learn discriminative representations, thus improving tracking robustness. Extensive experiments conducted on several challenging MOT benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, which achieves superior tracking performance.

title: A CQF Scheduling Algorithm based on ACO in Time-sensitive Networking
authors: Fei Fang, Hanghang Hou
abstract: The booming development of intelligent manufacturing has increasingly demanded scalability and flexibility of industrial networks. Dedicated industrial networks hinder industrial development due to rigid architecture and incompatible protocols, unable to support low-cost production adjustments and cross-unit collaboration. Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) delivers device interconnection, flexible adaptation and low-latency transmission with standardized Ethernet, dynamic resource allocation and deterministic scheduling, and is regarded as the optimal solution. As one of the core scheduling mechanisms, Cyclic Queuing and Forwarding (CQF) has become the focus of current research due to its advantages of concise scheduling logic, low implementation cost, and predictable latency. Although existing heuristic scheduling algorithms for CQF can quickly provide feasible solutions, the quality of the solutions depends on their fitness to the problem, which may lead to local optima. In this study, we propose a CQF Scheduling Algorithm based on ACO (ACO-CQF). The algorithm utilizes a spatiotemporal 2-dimensional optimization framework based on coupled core decision variables, achieving joint optimization of multi-path selection and injection slot allocation through roulette wheel selection and pheromone guidance. The experimental results show that ACO-CQF outperforms traditional heuristic algorithms in terms of scheduling success rate and resource utilization rate, while the growth rate of its time consumption is within an acceptable range.

title: Tight Bounds on Polynomials and Its Application to Dynamic Optimization Problems
authors: Eduardo M. G. Vila, Eric C. Kerrigan, Paul Bruce
abstract: This paper presents a pseudo-spectral method for Dynamic Optimization Problems (DOPs) that allows for tight polynomial bounds to be achieved via flexible sub-intervals. The proposed method not only rigorously enforces inequality constraints, but also allows for a lower cost in comparison with non-flexible discretizations. Two examples are provided to demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed method to solve optimal control problems. Solutions to the example problems exhibited up to a tenfold reduction in relative cost.

title: A comprehensive study on the quality of commercially available medium voltage surge arresters_supp1-3678375.docx
authors: Kuba Wojcik

title: Air Target Classification Using High-Resolution Range Profiles and Deep/Machine Learning Techniques
authors: Sedat Türe, Selçuk Paker
abstract: High-resolution range profiles (HRRPs) offer detailed structural information about air targets, making them crucial for classification and identification in both military and civilian contexts. This paper presents a comprehensive study on air target classification utilizing HRRP data through various deep learning (DL) and machine learning (ML) techniques. The research focuses on classifying five distinct missiles (AGM-65 Maverick, AGM-84 Slam ER, AGM-114 Hellfire, AGM-119 Penguin, AIM-7 Sparrow) and three fighter jet configurations (including Eurofighter, F-22 Raptor, Su-57 Felon, Mig-29 Fulcrum, and T-38 Talon), as well as common aircraft platforms (An-26, Yak-42, and Cessna Citation S/II). The study employs simulated HRRP data generated from 3D CAD models using both C-band (5520 MHz) and X-band (10 GHz) radar parameters with bandwidths ranging from 400 MHz to 600 MHz. The simulation approach's validity is confirmed by comparing generated HRRP data with published measured data for common aircraft. Experimental results indicate that deep learning classifiers generally outperform hybrid DL-ML methods in terms of average accuracy, with pre-processing steps further improving performance. Detailed analysis highlights the significant impact of target features, radar parameters, noise conditions, and target physical characteristics on classification accuracy, noting that larger targets typically yield better results. Classification performance was evaluated using standard metrics such as precision, recall, and the F1-score. The results support the integration of HRRP-based deep learning classifiers for rapid, reliable identification of diverse airborne threats, illustrating their potential as force multipliers in both civil and military radar systems.

title: Step-Aware Cross-View Transformer for Unsupervised Semiconductor Manufacturing Equipment Anomaly Detection_supp1-3677542.pdf
authors: Sangwook Park

title: Efficient Sleep Staging With Bayesian Uncertainty-Guided Active Learning
authors: Tianyou Yu, Rui Huang, Fei Wang, Jun Zhang, Wei Wu, Zhuliang Yu, Yuanqing Li, Jun Xiao, Zhenghui Gu
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Automated sleep staging is essential for large-scale and home-based sleep monitoring; however, in routine clinical practice, sleep annotation remains largely dependent on experienced experts performing time-consuming and labor-intensive manual scoring. Existing automatic systems often struggle to adapt reliably to new subjects, limiting their clinical adoption and reinforcing the reliance on expert review. This creates a strong demand for adaptive and efficient sleep staging systems that can substantially reduce annotation workload while preserving expert-level accuracy. We propose BayesSleepNet, a novel framework that integrates Bayesian uncertainty quantification with active learning for adaptive sleep staging. BayesSleepNet employs principled Bayesian modeling by placing distributions over network weights and performing Monte Carlo sampling at inference, enabling explicit quantification of model (epistemic) uncertainty. These uncertainty estimates drive a two-stage sample selection strategy that first fine-tunes the model using representative epochs and subsequently prioritizes persistently uncertain samples for expert review. Across four public sleep datasets, BayesSleepNet consistently improves performance—by 7.60% in accuracy, 8.27% in macro-F1, and 0.104 in Cohen's <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\kappa$</tex-math></inline-formula>—while requiring manual annotation of only 20% of data from new subjects. Despite its adaptive learning capability, BayesSleepNet remains computationally lightweight, using substantially fewer parameters than representative high-capacity state-of-the-art models. These results demonstrate the clinical promise of uncertainty-aware active learning as a practical and cost-efficient paradigm for semi-automated sleep staging. Code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/yuty2009/bayesugal</uri>.

title: Methodological Advances in Uncertainty-Aware Spectral Modeling for Raman Spectroscopy in Biological Systems
authors: Matias Heikkinen, Pasi Luukka
publish: IEEE Transactions on Molecular, Biological, and Multi-Scale Communications
abstract: Reliable in-field plant health monitoring using Raman spectroscopy remains challenging due to substantial measurement uncertainty, intensity fluctuations, and uncontrolled environmental conditions. Traditional chemometric approaches typically rely on mean comparisons between predefined treatment groups, making them unsuitable for practical diagnostics where only healthy reference data and unknown samples are available. To overcome this limitation, we introduce a heuristic, uncertainty-aware, semi-supervised anomaly-detection framework for Raman spectral analysis that focuses on relative spectral shape rather than absolute intensities, thereby minimizing the need for preprocessing steps such as spectral normalization. The proposed model infers spectral deviations by comparing the empirical distribution of pairwise similarities between an unknown sample and control spectra to the natural variability within the control group. This approach enables plant health detection directly from spectral similarity without requiring parametric statistical assumptions or fixed class definitions. Experimental results on basil plants exposed to different metal sulfate treatments demonstrate that the proposed method effectively separates pollutant-induced spectral deviations while correctly identifying the majority of healthy unknown samples.

title: Looking Ahead
authors: Craig Baehr

title: AlGaN/GaN Sidewall Gated HEMTs
authors: Rian Guan, Yixin Xiong, Xiaojun Zheng, Yi-shuo Huang, James S. Speck, Thomas N. Jackson, Rongming Chu
abstract: This letter reports the concept and implementation of a novel AlGaN/GaN sidewall gated HEMT design. This new device structure can achieve a high threshold voltage, based on a p-GaN/AlGaN/GaN epitaxy stack, by forming a MOS gate on the p-GaN sidewall. Incorporation of composition grading in the AlGaN layer eliminates electron injection barrier between the vertical channel on the p-GaN sidewall and the lateral 2DEG channel, resulting in favorable output I-V characteristics. Experimental implementation of this new device design resulted in a high threshold voltage of 3.5 V, favorable on-state I-V characteristics, low value of Cgd, breakdown voltage of 1050 V, and controlled degradation of dynamic on-resistance. Further improvements in on-resistance can be expected by optimizing device geometries; and further improvements in breakdown voltage and dynamic on-resistance can be expected with improved charge balance in the drift region.

title: Harnessing Pre-Course Aptitude Tests to Predict Performance on an Introductory Programming Assessment in Higher Education

title: Real-Time SIL Validation of Dual-Stage Robust Predictive Control for Multiphase Wind Generators
authors: Amel Kasri, Soufian Khettab, Youcef Belkhier, Kamel Ouari, Yassine Amirat, Mohamed Benbouzid
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Power Electronics and their Applications (ICPEA)
abstract: The increasing complexity and variability of wind energy conversion systems necessitate robust advanced control strategies to maintain optimal performance under changing conditions. This paper presents a novel two-stage robust predictive control strategy for dual-star induction generators in modern wind energy systems. An optimized voltage vector selection algorithm is developed to control both inverters while considering system constraints. This formulation ensures accurate selection of the converter switching state at each sampling instant. Moreover, a two-step-ahead predictive scheme is incorporated to compensate for actuation delays. The second stage focuses on speed regulation through a newly formulated robust cost function with dual integral action. The efficacy of the suggested control strategy is validated by software-in-the-loop testing conducted on the OPAL-RT platform. The results show that the developed DSRPC technique is efficient and robust, making it a suitable solution for high-performance DSIG.

title: AI-Driven Multi-Criteria Material Selection and Safety Analysis for Sustainable Building Design in India
authors: Jas Wadhwa, Sujeet Singh Bhadouria, Pooja Kumari
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Innovation for Sustainability (EmergIN)
abstract: This paper presents an AI-driven multi-criteria framework for selecting sustainable building materials in the Indian context, integrating environmental and safety considerations. We develop a hybrid model that combines embodied carbon (CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>) impacts, occupant evacuation safety, and seismic/soil resilience into a composite material suitability score. The model utilizes a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) classifier trained on a dataset of Indian construction scenarios (materials: concrete, steel, timber, fly-ash bricks, etc.). Features include material properties (e.g., embodied energy/carbon from the IFC India database), site seismic zone and soil type, building occupancy and height, and simulated evacuation times. We present three data tables: material properties (energy, carbon, strength), example scenario parameters, and classification performance metrics. The paper provides a full runtime/complexity analysis of each model component (linear regression for evacuation prediction, neural network for scoring, and a genetic algorithm-based optimization module). We benchmark our hybrid model against baseline ML classifiers (logistic regression, random forest) and a simple rule-based classifier using accuracy, precision, recall, F1, and ROC/AUC. Our hybrid model achieves near-perfect accuracy (≈0.98–0.99) and AUC≈1.0, outperforming baselines on the test set. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of integrating multi-criteria data in an AI model for sustainable material selection. Future work will extend this approach with sensor fusion and autonomous systems (e.g., IoT building sensors, robotic inspectors) and examine generalization to diverse climates and usage patterns.

title: Interpretable Deep Learning Framework for Electroencephalography-Based Language Decoding
authors: Nareddy Koushik Reddy, Karthik Reddy Padhira, D. Shiva Mani, N S S S Girish Kumar, M.A. Jabbar
publish: 2025 International Conference On Emerging Computation and Information Technologies (ICECIT)
abstract: For real-world brain-computer interface applications, it is still very difficult to reliably translate noisy and poor signal-to-noise electroencephalography (EEG) inputs into comprehensible verbal output. The end-to-end EEG-driven language interface presented in this paper ensures interpretability and real-time usefulness while decoding raw multichannel EEG recordings into coherent text and speech. The proposed strategy makes use of a Seq2Seq decoder for language production, a Residual BiLSTM encoder for temporal learning, and PSD characteristics from EEG signals. A language refinement layer is then added to enhance grammatical structure and readability. While an Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) module using occlusion sensitivity, permutation importance, and frequency-band relevance analysis offers clear insight into model decisions, post-processing and calibration modules improve prediction stability. The entire system is implemented as an interactive web-based interface that includes reporting, speech synthesis, and visualization features. This work’s primary contribution is a PSD-based pipeline that combines XAI for transparent EEG interpretation with residual BiLSTM–Seq2Seq decoding.The usefulness of the suggested design for useful EEG-to-language translation is validated by experimental evaluation, which shows character-level decoding accuracies is around 70–80% and word-level accuracies of roughly 30–40%.

title: Students' Perceptions of Baamboozle as a Game-based Learning Platform in Enhancing Students' Engagement and Motivation in EFL Classroom
authors: Ika Yuliani
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Modern Educational Technology (ICMET)
abstract: Game-based learning (GBL) has emerged as an effective strategy in English as a Foreign Language (EFL) instruction, as it provides interactive and motivating environments that foster students' engagement and active participation. Among various platforms, Baamboozle offers teambased activities that align with student-centered learning principles and can be adapted to different classroom needs. Despite its increasing use, limited research has examined students' perceptions of Baamboozle in secondary EFL contexts. To address this gap, the present study investigated how junior high school students in Yogyakarta, Indonesia perceived Baamboozle with regard to motivation, collaboration, and language development. A mixed-methods descriptive design was employed, with data collected from 58 students through a questionnaire combining Likert-scale and open-ended items. The results indicated that students generally perceived Baamboozle positively, reporting that it enhanced their enjoyment, motivation, and confidence during English lessons. They also highlighted its collaborative benefits, as the platform encouraged teamwork, peer interaction, and friendly competition. In addition, students perceived that Baamboozle contributed to the development of their English skills, particularly vocabulary acquisition, grammar comprehension, and overall learning confidence. These findings suggest that Baamboozle may serve as a promising supplementary tool to enrich EFL classrooms by promoting both engagement and language learning.

title: Deep Learning Architectures for Liver Segmentation: A Comparative Evaluation on the Duke Dataset
authors: Sarika Srivastava, Alok Kumar Srivastava, Chandan Kumar Shukla, Kusum Rawat, Manish Mishra
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical and Electronics Engineering (ICE3)
abstract: Precise segmentation of the liver from radiologic imaging data improves diagnostic precision, dictates treatment strategies, and directs surgical procedures. We present here a thorough comparative evaluation of current state-of-the-art deep learning-based semantic segmentation models, such as U-Net, U-Net++, DeepLabV3+, Feature Pyramid Network (FPN), Pyramid Scene Parsing Network (PSPNet), LinkNet, Pyramid Attention Network (PAN), Multi-Attention Network (MANet), and U-Net combined with EfficientNet (U-Net-EfficientNet). All models were trained and validated on the Duke Liver Segmentation Dataset, which is available on request. The Model performance indicators include evaluation metrics such as Dice Similarity Coefficient, Intersection over Union (IoU), pixel-wise accuracy, precision, recall, validation loss, and test loss. The U-Net with encoder emerges with EfficientNet is the best segmentation performance among the models evaluated, with a Dice Score of 0.9319 and an IoU of 0.8737 on the test set. These results highlight the efficacy of hybrid encoder-decoder models, especially those that utilize light backbone architectures, in improving liver segmentation quality. The findings from this comparison study can guide the choice and application of reliable deep learning models in the clinical imaging of livers.

title: Modeling Motivation Drift in Senior High School Students During Oral Communication Practice: Performance Evaluation of a Hidden Markov Model
authors: Angelo C. Arguson, Elisa V. Malasaga, Ace C. Lagman, Ronel F. Ramos
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Modern Educational Technology (ICMET)
abstract: Motivation drift, defined as the gradual decline of student engagement during learning tasks, poses a persistent challenge in sustaining effective instruction. This study evaluates the use of a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) to detect motivation drift among 120 Grade 11 Humanities and Social Sciences (HUMSS) students from three schools in Manila, Philippines. A hybrid developmental and prescriptive design guided the creation of an Intelligent Tutoring System (ITS) prototype, which logged behavioral features such as task accuracy, response time, and hint requests. The HMM was benchmarked against Logistic Regression, Random Forest, and a lightweight LSTM model. Results show that the HMM achieved an AUC of 0.869, Accuracy of 96.0%, and the best Brier Score (0.073), with low detection delay (1.4 tasks) and a 2.1% false alarm rate. Strong generalization was observed through Leave-One-Student-Out (LOSO) validation (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{AUC}=0.704$</tex>). Feature importance analysis identified Time on Task and Correctness as key predictors of motivational dynamics. While inferred states correlated strongly with self-reported motivation (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r=0.957$</tex>), reliance on unimodal behavioral logs limits ecological validity. Future work should integrate multimodal data (e.g., facial expressions, voice tone) and address challenges in data ethics and computational cost through lightweight feature extraction and privacy safeguards. These findings confirm that HMMs are effective for real-time modeling of motivational dynamics in ITS environments, while highlighting opportunities for multimodal extensions and advanced sequential models such as Transformers and enhanced LSTMs.

title: From Urban Data to Safer Cities: Predicting and Preventing Crime Using Digital Shadows
authors: Naveen Kilari, R Maranan, S. Kaliappan, Krishna Priya Burra
publish: 2025 International Conference on Data, Energy and Communication Networks (DECoN)
abstract: Cities are getting smarter and smarter, gathering data on surveillance, social dynamics, geography, and policing history. Building on this trove of data, digital shadows, which are virtual representations of urban environments, can conduct predictive policing via the simulation of crime patterns, environmental conditions, and human behavior in real time. These models help urban planners and law enforcement agencies make proactive decisions and anticipate probable crime by analyzing socioeconomic, geographic, temporal, and historical crime data. A hybrid ensemble classifier with Random Forest (RF) and Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGB) is suggested to improve the predictive accuracy and is able to address data noise and imbalance. RF is useful in controlling variability in features, whereas XGB uses incremental prediction errors. Tests run on actual criminal activity in urban areas indicate that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{RF}+\mathbf{GB}$</tex> model is more superior than the other classifiers with an accuracy of 95% as compared to Decision Tree (90%) and the Support Vector Machine (SVM) (88%). Although both DT and SVM are explanatory, they are not as robust and flexible as the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{RF}+\mathbf{GB}$</tex> ensemble.
