To address these limitations, we propose spatio-temporal causal learning with delay annotation (STCLD), which introduces a spatio-temporal delay attention (STDA) module to explicitly learn delay annotated spatio temporal causal graphs for soft sensing. STDA minimizes a maximum mean discrepancy objective to discover causal relations with edge specific delays, while attention path strength and multidimensional dynamic complexity are used to infer causal directions in a model-based way. The learned causal graph and delay information then guide a delay aware prediction module to build a self-interpretable soft sensor. Experiments on two real-world industrial datasets show that STCLD consistently outperforms strong baselines in both predictive accuracy and causal interpretability, providing a robust and general framework for interpretable soft sensor modeling in complex process industries.

title: TF-ProFM: Prototype-Based Time–Frequency Foundation Model for Generalizable Industrial Bearing Fault Diagnosis
authors: Songyan Pi, Bojian Chen, Xinmin Zhang, Zhihuan Song
abstract: The development of generalizable foundation models for intelligent bearing fault diagnosis is critical for modern industrial systems, yet it is severely hindered by the challenge of handling multisource, heterogeneous data. Existing methods often struggle to adapt to varying operating conditions and diverse signal characteristics. To address this issue, we propose a novel prototype-based time–frequency foundation model (TF-ProFM) that leverages self-supervised pretraining and few-shot fine-tuning. TF-ProFM features a time–frequency embedding module that uses random patching and local fast Fourier transform to process diverse signals into unified spectral representations. For pretraining, it employs a prototype-based contrastive learning objective, which explicitly encourages the model to learn a feature space with a clear cluster structure that aligns with downstream fault classification tasks. Extensive experiments on nine public datasets validate the superiority of our approach. In rigorous cross-dataset evaluations, TF-ProFM achieves the state-of-the-art fault diagnosis performance, outperforming existing methods even when fine-tuned with 1% of the labeled data from the target domain.

title: Background-Noise-Driven Detection of Diffusion-Generated Images
authors: Jiaming Li, Weihua Ou, Deyu Meng, Yong Xu
abstract: Diffusion-based image generation has proliferated, making robust detection of synthetic imagery critical. We propose a background-noise-driven detector motivated by the observation that real camera images preserve physical noise traces, whereas model-generated images tend to exhibit algorithm-induced residual statistics. Starting from sRGB, we apply a deterministic inverse ISP to obtain an approximate Bayer RAW representation, train a lightweight RAW-domain denoiser, and extract a noise residual by subtracting the denoised reconstruction from the inverse-ISP signal. A detector trained only on ADM residuals generalizes in a zero-shot manner to a wide range of unseen diffusion generators. By emphasizing noise-domain cues rather than semantic content, the proposed method offers a simple and practical approach to diffusion-generated image detection.

title: A Multi-Model Deep Learning Technique for Detecting Malicious Network Traffic
authors: Ogechukwu Okonor, Fakhreldin Saeed, Obinwa Ozonze
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Computer, Software and Modeling (ICCSM)
abstract: Digital transformation and network transactions have brought about more complex network connectivity and complexity in controlling network traffic, leading to a significant rise in network vulnerabilities. Hence, it opens a gap in networking control strategy, which needs to be equated to rapid digital advancement and the impending numerous malicious attacks. Therefore, adopting proactive, intelligent mechanisms to monitor and manage network communication is imminent. In this paper, deep learning techniques are embedded in our chosen intrusion detection system to monitor and track malicious network traffic proactively. Hence, based on the network behaviour and certain set thresholds, an alert will be triggered while gathering transactions and managing the network activities by event and time. This proposed model then collects the dataset and trains the network to detect transactional traffic flow toward alertness and an efficient detection strategy for network anomalies. This technique will be used on different datasets with a defined malicious traffic suspicion perimeter to evaluate the impact of our proposed technique in mitigating vulnerabilities that led to network anomaly behaviour.

title: Thermal Consequences of the 5GHz Wi-Fi Frequency Electromagnetic Field on Humans
authors: Tamar Nozadze, Giorgi Ghvedashvili, Vera Jeladze
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Computer, Software and Modeling (ICCSM)
abstract: The objective of this study is to investigate the thermal effects caused by human exposure to 5 GHz WI-FI electromagnetic fields (EMF) based on electromagnetic and thermal modeling using the finite difference time domain-FDTD method based software - FDTDLab. We analyzed the human whole body EMF exposure by calculating the Specific Absorption Rate (SAR) and the temperature increase due to the absorption of EMF energy by body tissues. We calculated values for 1g and 10g averaged SAR, whole body averaged SAR, and subsequent tissue temperature increase. The resulting SAR and temperature increase values were evaluated for compliance with international standards and regulations.

title: Detecting Test Case Smells Using Prompt Engineering: An Empirical Study in Industry
authors: Lennon Chaves, Flávia Oliveira, Leonardo Tiago
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Computer, Software and Modeling (ICCSM)
abstract: In the software testing process, test cases are developed to verify whether the software functions as intended. Consequently, test cases must be comprehensible to those who will execute them, and to ensure that they are written comprehensively and accurately, Large Language Models (LLMs) can be employed to assist with this task. With the aim of recommending issues within test cases, this study developed a prompt with 12 smells that should be avoided in test cases to facilitate comprehension. To evaluate the prompt, an empirical study was conducted with 5 participants from a software test team, employing the prompt on test cases from the requirements validation scope of a real industrial scenario. During the study, for each test case, the participants recorded the number of smells recommended by the LLM and subsequently indicated whether they agreed, partially agreed, or disagreed with the response obtained for each test case. Among the results of this experiment, the detection of “Misplaced verification” was observed in 90% of the utilized test cases, while no test case exhibited any “Spell check” smells. Another finding was that approximately 63% of responses from the LLM were classified as “Agree” or “Partially agree” by the participants regarding the smells detected in the test cases, indicating that improvements are necessary for these test cases. Thus, this empirical study presents the results of applying a prompt with 12 test case smells to recommend issues in test cases that validate the requirements of carriers.

title: PPDFP: Privacy-Preserving Data Filtering Protocol for Large-Scale ID Alignment of Vertical Federated Learning
authors: Zhengtao Jiang, Hanyu Zhou, Zhengyang Wu, Shujia Li, Yiyang Peng, Chenqing Cai
publish: 2025 13th International Conference on Communications and Broadband Networking (ICCBN)
abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without raw-data sharing, but its performance hinges on efficient alignment of vertically partitioned samples. Private set intersection (PSI) is the standard tool for this task; however, when sets reach millions or billions of records, the computational and communication burden of PSI becomes prohibitive, especially if the true intersection is sparse. We present the Privacy-Preserving Data Filtering Protocol (PPDFP), a Paillier-homomorphic preprocessing layer that identifies and discards non-overlapping intervals before the heavyweight PSI phase. PPDFP privately determines interval-overlap relations and computes candidate intersection ranges without revealing plaintext endpoints. Experiments on datasets of size 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">20</sup>-2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">24</sup> elements demonstrated that equipping PSI with PPDFP reduced the runtime by 19%-79% and trimmed communication to 0.07%-1.5% of the baseline. These gains empirically demonstrate that PPDFP provides a practical and scalable privacy-preserving preprocessing framework for largescale private set intersection.

title: Self - Organizing LoRa Network Based Ocean Observation Platform
authors: Shiqiang Wang, Xuan Geng, Yanli Xu
publish: 2025 13th International Conference on Communications and Broadband Networking (ICCBN)
abstract: At present, the ocean observation platform has the problems of low real-time performance, weak data interaction and poor ad hoc network ability, which cannot meet the observation needs of the marine environment. To adapt to the marine environment, this paper proposes an ocean observation platform system based on STM32 and LoRa network. The marine data collected by the LoRa terminal is transmitted to the gateway, and then transferred to the local core network through the cloud for data processing. The star network topology of multiple LoRa terminals and gateways is realized, which gets rid of using LoRaWAN gateway in the traditional LoRa ad hoc network system. Our solution provides a new concept for offshore observation platforms, which is low-cost, has strong self-organizing network capabilities and offers a wide communication coverage area. Under conditions of low power, short antenna, and close range without obstruction, the overall packet loss rate is only 1.6 %. When there is obstruction, the overall packet loss rate is 5.4%,which has a good performance.

title: Table of Contents

title: MMS-DETR: Fusing State Space Model and Mixed Aggregation for Small Object Detection on USVs
authors: Jizhang Luo, Feng Liu
publish: 2025 13th International Conference on Communications and Broadband Networking (ICCBN)
abstract: Unmanned Surface Vehicles(USVs) are playing an increasingly crucial role in key areas such as marine monitoring and maritime search and rescue. The accurate and realtime detection of small water-surface objects, such as persons overboard and small obstacles, is a core technical bottleneck for achieving autonomous navigation. Currently, this task faces severe challenges, including minute target scales, sparse features, and complex dynamic background interference from waves and reflections. To address these issues, this paper proposes MMS-DETR, an end-to-end network specifically designed for small object detection on the water surface. First, we designed the MambaVision Cross Stage Partial(M-CSP) module in the backbone network, which integrates the powerful long-range dependency modeling capability of the Vision State Space Model (MambaVision) with the efficient feature reuse concept of the CSP architecture. While maintaining high computational efficiency, this integration significantly enhances the model's perception of global context, thereby more effectively distinguishing minuscule targets from complex backgrounds. Second, we introduced a Mixed Aggregation RepC (MA-RepC) module to optimize the multi-scale feature fusion in the neck. This module deeply integrates a mixed aggregation network, Faster-Block, and a convolutional gated unit, improving the robustness of feature representation while effectively preventing the loss of small target information. Experimental results on the Flow-Image dataset show that, compared to the original RT-DETR-r18, the proposed model improves mAP @ 0.5 by 3.7% and mAP @ 0.5: 0.95 by 3.1%, while reducing parameters and computational cost by 19% and 5. 6%, respectively. It also demonstrates superior performance in small object detection on water surfaces compared to other mainstream algorithms.

title: Design and Implementation of Pulse Signal Analyzer Function on Spectrum Analyzer
authors: Xinghai Liu, Xiangdong Cui, Yuan Ou, Ying Zhao, Peipei Fang
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Vision, Image and Signal Processing (ICVISP)
abstract: Complex pulse modulated signals are widely used in radar, electronic countermeasure and other weapons and equipment. It is necessary to measure and analyze the pulse modulated signals efficiently and comprehensively in order to accurately evaluate the performance of radar systems and other equipment. This paper designs and realizes the pulse signal analysis function based on the spectrum analyzer. Through the research on the measurement principle, pulse data processing and display processing flow of the pulse signal analysis function, the overall implementation scheme of the pulse analysis function is designed. This scheme adopts an independent plug-in structure, through the combination of Server service process and UI display process, and through shared memory to read parameter status, transfer and obtain display results, to achieve the separation of business logic and display interface, and successfully applies the pulse signal analysis function to the spectrum analyzer software platform, achieving good results.

title: Hardware-Agnostic Quantum Kernel Feature Mapping for Anomaly Detection in Critical Infrastructure: A Cross-Testbed Validation on NISQ Processors
authors: Shujaatali Badami
abstract: Modern Industrial Control Systems (ICS) face sophisticated cyber-physical attacks that exploit nonlinear correlations between process variables, rendering traditional linear classifiers ineffective. This paper presents a hardware-agnostic Quantum Support Vector Machine (QSVM) framework employing an 8-qubit ZZFeatureMap kernel for anomaly detection in critical water treatment and thermal power infrastructure. Performance benchmarks were established via noise-free statevector simulation to determine the theoretical quantum kernel performance ceiling. Physical realizabilitywas separately validated via circuit execution on IBM’s 156-qubit ibm_fez processor, with completed hardware jobs enabling quantitative analysis of the simulation-to-hardware performance gap. Through rigorous cross-testbed validation on the SWaT (Secure Water Treatment) and HAI (Hardware-in-the-Loop Augmented ICS) datasets, our simulated approach achieves an AUC-ROC of 0.9912 ± 0.004 on SWaT and 0.8309 ± 0.050 on HAI, demonstrating consistent quantum kernel improvement of +10.8% AUC over classical RBF-kernel SVMs on the more challenging HAI testbed. Statistical robustness is established through 5-seed cross-validation with stratified sampling. Hardware execution on ibm_fez confirms physical realizability with circuit depth 76 and 28 CNOT gates, while revealing an expected fidelity degradation of approximately 17–20% compared to ideal simulation due to gate errors and decoherence. The quantum kernel’s ZiZj entanglement structure geometrically captures pairwise feature correlations, providing inductive bias well-suited for coupled sensor dynamics. Unlike deep learning approaches that require massive attack datasets unavailable in critical infrastructure domains, our kernel method generalizes effectively from limited training samples. All experimental artifacts are publicly available at https://github.com/Ali-Badami/Quantum-IDS. IBM Quantum job identifiers: d5l9htjh36vs73bgsi3g (SWaT), d5l9huk8d8hc73cfb0pg (HAI).

title: HyPSI: A Hybrid Protocol Semantic Inference Framework for Industrial Control Systems
authors: Yuxiang Lei, Xuejun Zong, Kan He, Lian Lian, Yifei Sun, Plamen Vasilev, Bowei Ning
abstract: The convergence of Operational Technology (OT) and Information Technology (IT) exposes legacy Industrial Control Systems (ICS) devices to distinct protocol-level attacks due to the lack of semantic filtering mechanisms in modern edge gateways. However, effective security monitoring is hindered by the prevalence of proprietary, undocumented protocol implementations that create a semantic gap. Existing solutions struggle to generalize across vendor-specific implementations due to severe distribution shifts in protocol encoding and the prohibitive cost of manual annotation. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes HyPSI (Hybrid Protocol Semantic Inference), a framework that reformulates semantic inference not merely as a classification task, but as a domain adaptation problem. Operating on a knowledge-guided weakly supervised paradigm, HyPSI utilizes a Knowledge-Guided Semantic Mapper to transform constant-cost expert heuristics into high-confidence pseudo-labels, effectively addressing the initialization challenge with-out large-scale labeled data. Subsequently, to overcome the barrier of protocol heterogeneity, we introduce a Domain-Adaptive Contrastive Learning mechanism. This approach explicitly aligns the representation spaces of diverse protocols, extracting domain-invariant structural features that persist across proprietary variants. Experimental results on a high-fidelity Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) testbed demonstrate that HyPSI achieves an average inference accuracy of 88.4%. Crucially, the framework proves its practical utility in downstream security tasks: HyPSI-guided fuzzing significantly improves firmware code coverage compared to blind approaches, validating its effectiveness in automating vulnerability discovery for legacy industrial assets.

title: Navigating Crisis: How Government Support Shapes Operational Resilience
authors: Ning Ning You, Yitian Lou, XiaoPing Zhao, Pufan You, Pedro Chumpitaz Flores, Kaixun Hua
abstract: The unexpected crises create significant disruptions in business operations, prompting many companies to re-evaluate their operational strategies and explore new methods to enhance their resilience. While government support may offer greater flexibility and adaptability, its effectiveness in improving operational resilience during such disruptions remains understudied. This study aims to address this knowledge gap by examining the operational resilience of government-supported organizations before and after disasters in 2018 and 2023. It investigates the impact of government support on operational resilience through R&D inputs during these periods and explores the moderating role of dynamic capabilities. The findings indicate that government support has fostered operational resilience amid crises. In 2018, the positive effect of government support on operational resilience was more pronounced in organizations with higher levels of dynamic capabilities. By 2023, however, dynamic capabilities no longer significantly influenced the relationship between government support and operational resilience. Instead, R&D inputs mediated, strengthening the relationship between government support and enhanced operational resilience. These insights contribute to our understanding of improving operational resilience and provide valuable guidance for organizations seeking to leverage government support to bolster their resilience during disruptive times.

title: A High-Precision Wearable System for Gait Analysis Integrating High-Spatial-Resolution Pressure Insoles with Embedded Walking-Pattern-Informed Algorithms
authors: Yidan Wu, Ruoyu Sun, Shuaishuai Li, Kang Li, Yong Nie, Huizhi Wang, Tingrui Pan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Contemporary wearable devices for gait analysis still encounter precision limitations that preclude their qualification as medical practicing tools. This study presents a fidelity-tracking wearable system (FTW) for gait analysis with novel walking-pattern-informed algorithms leveraging pressure insoles integrated with inertial measurement unit (IMU). Customized pressure insoles achieved high spatial resolution (5.2 points/cm²) based on an interleaved network multiplexing electrode (INME) sensor design. Controlled experiments involving nine healthy subjects demonstrated that this design significantly improves the estimation accuracy of both pressure-related and temporal gait parameters. Walking-pattern-informed algorithms were developed to further enhance the accuracy of parameter estimation. Specifically, pressure and area thresholds were respectively used for detecting toe-off and strike moments, optimizing the accuracy of temporal parameter calculations. A gradually zeroing the pressure in the metatarsal region (GZPM) strategy was employed to mitigate bending noise at peak thrust, enhancing the accuracy of pressure parameter calculations. Additionally, IMU-determined toe-off timing was advanced by 25% to better align with typical gait characteristics and optimize step width calculations. A classification algorithm was developed based on four fundamental gait conditions to calculate clinically defined foot progression angles. FTW enables the simultaneous and precise output of 13 gait-related parameters. Compared to previous studies, errors in step time and stance phase were reduced by 49.4% and 36.2%, respectively. The root mean squared (RMS) error of the measured plantar pressure center trajectory length was 0.48 ± 0.48 cm, and the accuracy of the spatial parameters was consistent with previous studies. This high-precision, multi-parameter wearable gait monitoring system holds significant promise for home-based long-term monitoring of chronic musculoskeletal and neurological disorders.

title: Three Fundamental Decisions in the Evaluation of Autonomous Naval Mine Hunting Operations
authors: Timothy R. Hammond
abstract: This article examines three key decisions in evaluating autonomous mine hunting operations using the Mine Shape Bayes Net framework. These decisions concern the classification of mine-like contacts (MILCOs) in side-looking sonar imagery. The first asks which mine classes to consider. The second asks whether to calibrate assessments of MILCO classification performance with mine garden trials or target simulation techniques. The third asks about the optimal division of responsibility for classification between human operators and Artificial Intelligence (AI). Three options for this division are considered: human supervised, shared responsibility, and fusion (this last giving humans the smallest role). Simulation results show that fusion consistently yields the lowest mean sum squared error in estimating mine counts by shape, particularly under conditions of uncertain underwater navigation. When underwater navigation is perfect, human-centered approaches improve. It is not that AI is inherently superior, but rather that it is more robust to navigation uncertainty underwater, giving it an edge in current operational contexts.

title: Pre-Op Diagnostics of Power Switch in Three-Phase Full-Bridge Inverters
authors: Chung-Chia Wu, Le-Ren Chang-Chien, Hsuan Liang, Chun-Kai Liu
abstract: Wide-bandgap (WBG) semiconductors, such as silicon carbide (SiC) and gallium nitride (GaN), have demonstrated clear advantages in power electronics by enabling higher switching frequencies and improved efficiency under high system voltages. However, these benefits come with increased switching stress on power switches, leading to a higher risk of failure. To enhance system reliability, a timely health assessment of power switches before operation becomes critical. This paper presents a Pre-Operational (Pre-Op) Health Assessment Circuit designed for SiC-based power switches in a three-phase full-bridge inverter. Unlike conventional monitoring methods, the proposed approach performs diagnostics before the device is energized, allowing early detection of degradation. The design is theoretically based on the power switch aging characteristics, which serves as a foundation for pre-failure diagnostics. Because the diagnostic circuit is embedded inside the inverter system, the effects of printed circuit board (PCB) parasitics on monitoring accuracy are thoroughly investigated, addressing practical implementation challenges. Simulation and experimental results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed circuit in detecting the aging phenomena. The findings demonstrate that the Pre-Op Diagnostics provides an effective early-warning capability, enhancing the reliability of WBG-based inverter systems.
